title: The import Statement
subtitle: Use Python's vast standard library to avoid reinventing the wheel
learning_objectives: Use 'import module' to load a module
Use 'from module import name' to import specific items
Create an alias with 'import module as alias'
Understand what the Python Standard Library is

sections.heading: What is a Module?
sections.content: A module is a file containing Python code that you can reuse. Python comes with a huge collection of built-in modules called the Standard Library — tools for maths, random numbers, dates, file handling, and much more. You load a module with the import statement.
sections.code_example:
import math

# Now use things from math with dot notation
print(math.pi)
print(math.sqrt(16))
print(math.ceil(3.2))
print(math.floor(3.8))

sections.heading: from ... import and Aliases
sections.content: You can import specific items from a module so you don't need to type the module name every time. You can also create a shorter alias with as.
sections.code_example:
# Import just what you need
from math import pi, sqrt
print(pi)       # no need to write math.pi
print(sqrt(25)) # no need to write math.sqrt

# Create an alias
import math as m
print(m.factorial(5))  # 120

# from ... import with alias
from math import factorial as fact
print(fact(6))  # 720
sections.note: Avoid 'from module import *' — it imports everything and can cause confusing name clashes.

exercises.title: Import and Use math
exercises.prompt: Import the math module. Print math.pi rounded to 2 decimal places, and the square root of 144.
exercises.starter_code:
import math

# Print pi rounded to 2 decimal places
# Print sqrt of 144
hints: round(math.pi, 2) rounds pi to 2 decimal places
math.sqrt(144) gives the square root of 144

exercises.title: from import
exercises.prompt: Use 'from math import factorial, pow'. Print factorial(5) and pow(2, 10) (2 to the power of 10).
exercises.starter_code:
from math import factorial, pow

# Print factorial of 5
# Print 2 to the power of 10
exercises.solution:
from math import factorial, pow

print(factorial(5))
print(pow(2, 10))
hints: After 'from math import', use the names directly

quiz.question: After 'import math', how do you call the sqrt function?
quiz.explanation: After 'import math', you access functions using dot notation: math.sqrt(16). The module name comes first, then a dot, then the function name.

quiz.question: What does 'from math import pi' do differently from 'import math'?
options: It imports a different version of math
It lets you use pi directly without writing math.pi
It prevents other math functions from working
quiz.explanation: from math import pi brings pi directly into your namespace so you can use it as just 'pi' instead of 'math.pi'. The trade-off is that it can cause name clashes if you have your own variable called 'pi'.

completion_message: Great! You can now tap into Python's enormous Standard Library!
